dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond for Call Type: Convulsions/Seizure at 5446 Kendall Rd, [CITY].
Hello, I'm Jordan with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Our 3-year-old daughter had a seizure about ten minutes ago and hasn't been fully alert since.
Can you say your name for me, sweetheart?
I see she opened her eyes when I called her.
She didn't say anything, but her eyes followed my hand slightly.
She moved her arm slightly when I gently touched her foot, ma'am.
Airway is still patent with no signs of obstruction. Has there been any drooling or difficulty swallowing?
Her breathing was irregular during the seizure and still sounds labored now.
Her respirations are shallow and in an irregular pattern. I'll begin oxygen delivery.
SpO2 is 78% on room air, which is low. I'll start 3 liters via nasal cannula now.
SpO2 improved to 95% with 3L O2. Pulse is 119, strong and regular. Looks good.
Pupils are equal and reactive. Blood glucose is 232 mg/dL. She doesn't look pale or sweaty.
Respirations remain at 13 per minute, shallow and variable.
Blood pressure is 132 over 73. That's within a normal range for now.
SpO2 is 97% on 3L O2. We're not seeing any room for improvement in oxygen now.
I'm running 4-lead ECG monitoring now. Her rhythm is sinus tachycardia.
Blood glucose is high at 232 mg/dL. Has she had anything to eat or drink recently?
Pupils are 2mm, equal, and reactive. She has a rightward gaze with nystagmus-this is concerning.
Skin is evenly pink, no bruising or rashes. Any signs of burns or cuts?
Do you know of any medical issues she has?
Yes, she has epilepsy and cerebral palsy.
Any recent surgeries or additional medical history?
Cerebral palsy is diagnosed, but it does not appear to impact her legs at this time.
What medications is she taking currently?
She's on Keppra and Baclofen.
When did she take her last dose of Keppra?
She missed her morning dose and took her evening dose earlier today.
Did she vomit after taking her last dose today?
Yes, she vomited about ten minutes after receiving it.
On a scale of 0-10, could you assess your daughter for signs of pain?
She was very sleepy after the seizure, but I don't think she's in pain now.
Let me confirm blood glucose again-still 232 mg/dL.
Blood glucose of 232 is consistent with her last check.
Her respiratory rate is now 11 per minute, with a bit more depth compared to earlier.
Pulse is steady at 119 and SpO2 at 99%.
Her end-tidal CO2 improved from 84 down to 46. Breathing looks more regular now.
3L of oxygen continues via nasal cannula without difficulty.
I'm going to administer 2.5 mg of Midazolam (also known as Versed) intramuscularly to stop the seizure.
I'll give it in her left thigh muscle per protocol. It should take effect quickly.
I'll try to start an IV in one of her arms or legs per protocol.
First attempt in the right forearm was unsuccessful.
Second attempt at the left leg also failed. We will continue with IM medication per protocol.
We'll transport her to [COMPANY_NAME] as requested by the family. Is that okay?
She is safely on the stretcher and secured with the ACR.
Her breathing is shallow but no longer strained. Appears stable now.
We're following protocol 9-12 for a medical seizure.
Her mother is riding with us and is seated at the back with the harness for safety.
This is Unit 3 reporting a 3-year-old patient with post-seizure activity.
Initially, she had a rightward gaze with nystagmus and responded well to 2.5 mg Midazolam IM in the left thigh.
Oxygen and EKG monitoring were initiated. SpO2 improved from 78% to 95% after oxygen delivery.
Vitals on scene were pulse 119, respiratory rate 13, BP 132/73, and SpO2 97% on 3L oxygen.
Post-medical treatment, her SpO2 was 99%, with a respiratory rate of 25 and a stable blood glucose of 232.
Her GCS improved from approximately 5 to more responsive post-Midazolam. No signs of pain reported.
She has a known medical history of epilepsy and cerebral palsy. She is currently on Keppra and Baclofen.
She missed her morning dose of Keppra and vomited about ten minutes after her evening dose.
She is currently stable and en route to [CITY] with no further interventions needed at this time.
All relevant interventions are logged in EPCR and our protocols have been followed.
Her breathing is improving en route. I'll continue monitoring with the 4-lead during transport.
She is currently stable and prepared for transport. Ready when you are.
[CITY] is aware and has been alerted to the call.
I will continue 3L O2 and repeat vitals as needed.
Rechecking glucose remains high at 232, no change.
Pulse is 117, respirations are 11, with a blood pressure of 136/81.
